serving.description: 1 whole tandoori pomfret (approximately 400 g edible portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving is one whole tandoori pomfret fish, yielding approximately 400 g of edible flesh after cooking, typically served with sliced onions, lemon wedges, and mint chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Pomfret, raw and cooked; ICMR-NIN 2017 edition, adapted for tandoori preparation with marinade contribution
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports pomfret (Pampus argenteus) raw and steamed values. Tandoori preparation involves marination in yogurt and spices followed by high-heat tandoor cooking. Values adapted: protein and fat adjusted for cooking loss (~10% moisture loss), sodium elevated to reflect marinade salt and spice contribution (~350 mg/100 g estimated), saturated fat adjusted for yogurt marinade contribution. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Fish, pompano, cooked, dry heat' (FDC ID 175148) used as secondary cross-check for cooked fish macros.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021; analogous estimate for high-protein low-carbohydrate cooked fish dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori pomfret fish. Cooked fish and seafood dishes with negligible carbohydrate content are consistently assigned GI values of approximately 5 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, reflecting the near-absence of glycemic carbohydrates. Carbohydrate content per 100 g is 2.8 g (primarily from marinade spices and yogurt), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 0.6 per 400 g serving. GI set to 5 as the defensible analogous estimate for a high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked fish preparation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade and spice blend (~350 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the tandoori marinade contributes approximately 350 mg sodium per 100 g; a full 400 g serving provides ~1400 mg sodium, approaching the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and portion size should be moderated accordingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~5) and low carbohydrate content (2.8 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori pomfret fish is an excellent protein source with a very low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate load, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 polyunsaturated fat content and lean protein profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pomfret is a source of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein; the tandoori cooking method uses minimal added oil, making this dish a heart-friendly option, though the elevated sodium from the marinade warrants attention in patients with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Pomfret Fish is a classic Indian seafood preparation in which a whole pomfret (Pampus argenteus or related species) is marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture — typically containing red chilli powder, turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, garlic, lemon juice, and oil — and then cooked at high temperature in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The result is a fish with a lightly charred, smoky exterior, tender and moist flesh, and a complex spiced flavour profile that is simultaneously tangy, aromatic, and mildly hot. Pomfret is prized in Indian coastal and Singaporean Indian cuisine for its firm white flesh, relatively few bones, and mild natural sweetness that pairs well with bold tandoori spicing. Nutritionally, tandoori pomfret is a high-protein, moderate-fat food providing approximately 162 kcal per 100 g, with 22.5 g protein and 7.2 g fat. The fat profile is favourable, with polyunsaturated fatty acids including omega-3s predominating. Carbohydrate content is very low at 2.8 g per 100 g, conferring a glycemic index of approximately 5 and a negligible glycemic load. Sodium is elevated due to the marinade (~350 mg/100 g) and should be considered by individuals managing hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is typically served with sliced raw onions, lemon wedges, mint chutney, and sometimes a side of naan or roti. In Singapore, it is commonly found at Indian restaurants and is a popular choice for seafood lovers seeking a flavourful, lower-calorie alternative to fried fish preparations.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori pomfret?
Is tandoori pomfret good for diabetics?
What is the protein content of tandoori pomfret?
Can I eat tandoori pomfret if I have high blood pressure?
What spices are used in tandoori pomfret marinade?
What does tandoori pomfret taste like?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN provides pomfret raw and steamed values for Indian pomfret species; values have been adapted for tandoori preparation (marinade sodium, yogurt fat contribution, cooking moisture loss). No exact tandoori pomfret entry exists in any approved primary source. USDA FoodData Central used as secondary cross-check. Confidence is medium due to the adaptation required.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for pomfret, with adjustments for tandoori marinade contribution (sodium ~350 mg/100 g estimated from marinade salt and spice blend) and cooking moisture loss. USDA FoodData Central 'Fish, pompano, cooked, dry heat' (FDC ID 175148) used as secondary cross-check for cooked fish macros. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as pomfret is a marine fish with negligible industrial trans fat and the preparation uses no hydrogenated oils. GI set to 5 as analogous estimate for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked fish per University of Sydney GI Database conventions. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA cross-reference values for cooked pomfret-type fish; no sentinel -1 values remain as all fields have been estimated from approved sources. FoodOn URI used is the closest parent node for cooked pomfret; no exact tandoori pomfret node exists in FoodOn. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is the closest parent for cooked marine fish. SNOMED CT has no applicable prepared food concept for this dish.
